paper.title: Müntz-Szász Networks: Neural Architectures with Learnable Power-Law Bases
paper.abstract: Standard neural network architectures employ fixed activation functions (ReLU, tanh, sigmoid) that are poorly suited for approximating functions with singular or fractional power behavior, a structure that arises ubiquitously in physics, including boundary layers, fracture mechanics, and corner singularities. We introduce Müntz-Szász Networks (MSN), a novel architecture that replaces fixed smooth activations with learnable fractional power bases grounded in classical approximation theory. Each M

memory_insight.before_after_comparisons: MSN achieves 10x fewer parameters than MLPs

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Using learnable fractional power bases can significantly improve the accuracy of neural networks in approximating singular or fractional power functions
technical_details: Universal approximation from the Müntz-Szász theorem
Novel approximation rates: error O(|μ- α|^2) with a single learned exponent
analysis.deployment_feasibility: MSN can be deployed on laptops due to its reduced parameter count (10x fewer parameters than MLPs), but its actual deployment feasibility on mobile devices is uncertain due to the lack of specific memory usage and bandwidth requirements

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
